questions.question: What is a magnet?
questions.correct_answer: A magnet is a material that produces a magnetic field.

questions.question: What are the two poles of a magnet?
questions.correct_answer: The North pole and South pole

questions.question: How do magnets interact with each other?
questions.correct_answer: Magnets repel like poles and attract unlike poles.

questions.question: What is the Earth's magnetic field used for?
How can you identify a magnet without using another magnet?
questions.correct_answer: By observing its effect on other objects.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a magnetic compass?
Can all magnets be used to pick up metal objects?
questions.correct_answer: No, only strong magnets can do this.

questions.question: What is the difference between a magnet and an electromagnet?
questions.correct_answer: An electromagnet requires electricity to function

questions.question: How are magnets used in everyday life?
questions.correct_answer: In various applications such as motors, generators, and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) machines.

questions.question: What is the significance of magnetism in our daily lives?
questions.correct_answer: It has numerous practical applications and plays a crucial role in many industries.

important_notes.title: Properties of Magnets
important_notes.content: Magnets have two poles, North and South, which interact with each other to produce magnetic forces.

important_notes.title: Types of Magnets
important_notes.content: There are permanent magnets (e.g., iron) and temporary magnets (e.g., electromagnets).

important_notes.title: Magnetic Fields
important_notes.content: A magnetic field is a region around a magnet where the force of magnetism can be detected.

important_notes.title: Earth's Magnetic Field
important_notes.content: The Earth's magnetic field is used for navigation and has changed over time due to geological events.

important_notes.title: Magnetic Materials
important_notes.content: Some materials, like iron and nickel, are naturally magnetic, while others can be magnetized.

important_notes.title: Applications of Magnets
important_notes.content: Magnets have numerous practical applications in industries such as medicine, transportation, and energy production.

important_notes.title: Magnetic Forces
important_notes.content: Magnets exert attractive and repulsive forces on other magnets and magnetic materials.

important_notes.title: History of Magnetism
important_notes.content: The study of magnetism dates back to ancient civilizations, with early observations of lodestones and natural magnets.

important_notes.title: Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI)
important_notes.content: MRI machines use strong magnetic fields and radio waves to create detailed images of the body's internal structures.

important_notes.title: Electromagnets
important_notes.content: Electromagnets are temporary magnets created by coiling a wire around a core material and passing an electric current through it.

facts.fact: The Earth's magnetic field is thought to be generated by the movement of molten iron in its core.
Magnets can be used to levitate objects, a phenomenon known as magnetic levitation or maglev.
Some animals, such as birds and turtles, use magnetism to navigate during migration.
The first recorded observation of magnetism dates back to ancient Greece in the 6th century BCE.
Magnets have been used for medical purposes, including treating conditions such as arthritis and pain relief.
The strength of a magnet can be increased by using it in conjunction with other magnetic materials or electromagnets.
Magnetic fields are used to study the behavior of subatomic particles and forces in physics research.
Some types of rocks, such as lodestone, have natural magnetic properties.
Magnets can be used to detect and identify minerals and ores in geological surveys.
The study of magnetism has led to the development of new technologies, including electric motors and generators.
